title: He Kōrero o te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | The Stories of Our Stream: Understanding Narrative Through Place
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a vital waterway flowing through our rohe. They will engage with Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (traditional stories) about the stream, learn about its role in local history as a food source and transportation route, and examine contemporary issues affecting its health through both Māori and Pākehā perspectives.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau and contemporary accounts about the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to identify narrative elements and cultural perspectives.
Compose a piece of creative writing (e.g., a poem, short narrative, or diary entry) that gives a 'voice' to the stream, integrating descriptive language and themes learned from local stories.
Effectively present findings from a short interview with a community member about their connection to the stream, using clear and appropriate oral language.
Collaborate in a small group to create a visual representation (e.g., a story map or illustrated panel) that synthesizes a story about the stream with key environmental or historical facts.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero (A Walk, A Story)
activities.description: The class will take a guided hīkoi (walk) along a safe section of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with a local kaumātua or knowledge holder. Students will listen to pūrākau (traditional stories) associated with specific landmarks and record sensory details (sights, sounds, feelings) in a journal to use as inspiration for later writing.

activities.title: Ngā Reo e Rua | Two Voices of the Water
activities.description: In pairs, students will read and analyze two short texts: a traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about the stream and a recent article from the Hawke's Bay Today about a local stream restoration project. Using a Venn diagram, they will compare the purpose, audience, language features, and perspective of each text.

activities.title: Te Reo o te Awa | Voice of the Stream
activities.description: Using their journal notes from the hīkoi and the themes from the texts analyzed, students will draft a piece of creative writing from the perspective of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They will workshop their drafts in small groups, focusing on using powerful verbs and precise adjectives to create tone and atmosphere.

activities.title: He Kōrero ki te Taiao | Interviewing Our Community
activities.description: Students will develop 3-5 interview questions about personal connections to the local environment. They will interview a whānau or community member (e.g., a parent, grandparent, neighbour) and prepare a brief summary to share key insights with the class about how people value our local waterways.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website and digital archives for pūrākau
Local kaumātua or representative from Manaaki Te Awa restoration project
Hawke's Bay Knowledge Bank and Hawke's Bay Today digital archive
Sensory journals and art supplies for creating story maps

tasks: Completed Venn diagram comparing narrative and informational texts
Final draft of creative writing piece 'Voice of the Stream'
A short oral presentation (1-2 min) summarizing a community interview

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
Tikanga-ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people’s customs, traditions, and values.
